Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study maybe nine hours a week, scattered whenever I could fit it in between my part-time job and other stuff. Sometimes I'd cram reading into late nights, other times I'd go days without touching course materials. I know it's not ideal, but I just couldn't get myself motivated to set a proper schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, mostly driven by deadlines. If something was due, I'd suddenly find the energy to study. Otherwise, I'd drift and do other things. The only consistent thing was inconsistency, really. I kept telling myself I'd get more organized, but it never happened.
Q3: Usually it was because I remembered an assignment was coming up or I got a notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because that fear of falling behind would creep up on me. It wasn't really about wanting to learn—more about not wanting to fail or look stupid.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a real routine for me. There were stretches where I logged in more frequently, like 43 times over the course, but it felt reactive rather than habitual. Maybe toward the middle of the semester when assignments piled up, I checked in more regularly out of necessity. But it never felt natural or automatic.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful to me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and go through them at my own pace, highlighting stuff. The task checklists helped too because they showed me exactly what I needed to complete. Videos were okay but I found myself zoning out during them.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums entirely—zero posts from me. I just didn't see the point, and participating felt awkward and forced. Some of the supplementary readings I skipped too because I was already stretched thin with the required stuff. When you're low on resources and time, you have to pick what matters most.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd do it. Abstract stuff or things that seemed like busy work? I'd skip those. I needed to see a clear payoff because my motivation was already pretty low to begin with.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required ones I'd force myself through even when I didn't want to, but optional stuff got pushed aside immediately. There was always this calculation in my head—is this going to affect my grade? If not, it wasn't happening. I know I probably missed out on some good material.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download the readings and notes to my phone or laptop and study on the train to work or during breaks. I couldn't always afford data, so having things saved was essential. I also used some free YouTube videos and external resources that explained concepts better than the course materials sometimes.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying from printed notes or downloaded files where the LMS wouldn't register anything. I'd spend hours reviewing material on paper, rewriting key points because that's how I retain information. The system would just show me as inactive, but I was actually working through the content.
Q11: Definitely. I'd open a resource once, download it, and then never go back to that page again. The LMS would show one view, but I might have read that document five or six times offline. It was more practical for me given my internet situation isn't always reliable and I needed to study wherever I could.
Q12: There were times when I felt genuinely scared about keeping up, especially around midterms. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed instead of productive. When money got tight or work demanded more hours, the course became harder to prioritize. Those moments made me want to disengage completely, even though I knew I shouldn't.
Q13: Absolutely. Fatigue from working and competing responsibilities made the LMS feel like another burden. When I was exhausted, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely. The workload felt manageable on paper but overwhelming when life got complicated.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while doing something else, or I'd scroll through readings without really processing them. It was like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something. My mind was elsewhere, worried about bills or just too tired to focus properly.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts indefinitely and delayed starting assignments until the last possible moment. Part of it was low motivation—I just couldn't make myself care enough. Part of it was fear of doing it wrong and looking dumb. The mindfulness stuff they introduced helped a bit with the anxiety, but the avoidance habit was hard to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I'd drift completely. When a deadline approached, I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS, downloading everything, checking requirements multiple times. It wasn't healthy—lots of last-minute stress—but it's the only way I got things done.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback gave me a temporary boost, but it faded quickly. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that type of work in the future. I appreciated knowing where I stood, but it didn't fundamentally change how I engaged with the course. I'd note it and move on.
Q18: The structure helped in some ways because it was clear what was required and when. But it also felt rigid, like I had to engage on someone else's terms. The weekly modules kept me somewhat on track even when my motivation was low. Without that structure, I probably would have fallen apart completely.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement easier for someone in my situation. The clear task completion tracking helped me know what I'd done. What made it harder was the emphasis on discussion participation—I just couldn't bring myself to do that. Also, some modules felt disconnected from what actually mattered for assessments.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in, clicking around, maybe downloading stuff, but not actually producing work until the last minute. Or they could be anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just nervous energy or procrastination disguised as engagement.
Q21: That sounds familiar to me. My LMS activity wasn't super high relative to some people, but I still got a Merit grade. A lot of my actual learning happened offline with downloaded materials. If you just looked at my login patterns or video watch time, you'd miss that I was reading and writing notes on my own time.
Q22: Logs can show when you accessed things and how often, which gives a surface picture of engagement. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you actually understood anything or were just clicking through. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or whether someone is genuinely learning versus just surviving.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real mental effort into understanding it. For me, I was rarely truly engaged—I was mostly just trying to get through and not fail. Real engagement would mean being curious and wanting to explore beyond the requirements. I didn't have that this semester.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I felt most of the time, or how much I studied offline because of limited resources. It wouldn't capture the mental effort of just showing up when my motivation was rock bottom. It also wouldn't show that the mindfulness intervention actually helped me manage my anxiety somewhat, even if my engagement patterns didn't change dramatically.
